was the third tier of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system") in [1920–21](/wiki/1920%E2%80%9321_Football_League "1920–21 Football League") and again from [1958](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_Football_League "1958–59 Football League") until [1992](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Football_League "1991–92 Football League"). When the [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") was formed, the Third Division become the fourth tier of English football. In 2004, following the formation of the [Football League Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship"), the division was renamed [Football League Two](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two").

Cox, Richard; Russell, Dave; Vamplew, Wray (24 July 2020). [*Encyclopedia of British Football*](https://books.google.com/books?id=1WDwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA345). Routledge. p. 345. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-000-14414-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-000-14414-7 "Special:BookSources/978-1-000-14414-7").

The **Football League Third Division** was the third tier of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system") in [1920–21](/wiki/1920%E2%80%9321_Football_League "1920–21 Football League") and again from [1958](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_Football_League "1958–59 Football League") until [1992](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Football_League "1991–92 Football League"). When the [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") was formed, the Third Division become the fourth tier of English football. In 2004, following the formation of the [Football League Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship"), the division was renamed [Football League Two](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two").

## Founder clubs of the Third Division (1920)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Football_League_Third_Division&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Founder clubs of the Third Division (1920)")]

Most of these clubs were drawn from what was then the top division of the [1919–20 Southern Football League](/wiki/1919%E2%80%9320_Southern_Football_League "1919–20 Southern Football League"), in an expansion of the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") south of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"). As [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") was long considered a potential entrant for the Second Division due to their [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") exploits and good Southern League performances, they were sent directly into the Second Division and Grimsby Town, who finished in last place in the Second Division in 1919–20, were relegated.

* [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C.")
* [Brighton & Hove Albion](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C. "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C.")
* [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.")
* [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") (inaugural champions in 1920–21)
* [Exeter City](/wiki/Exeter_City_F.C. "Exeter City F.C.")
* [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C.")
* [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.")
* [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C.")
* [Merthyr Town](/wiki/Merthyr_Town_F.C._(1909) "Merthyr Town F.C. (1909)")
* [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.")
* [Newport County](/wiki/Newport_County_A.F.C. "Newport County A.F.C.")
* [Northampton Town](/wiki/Northampton_Town_F.C. "Northampton Town F.C.")
* [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.")
* [Plymouth Argyle](/wiki/Plymouth_Argyle_F.C. "Plymouth Argyle F.C.")
* [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.")
* [Queens Park Rangers](/wiki/Queens_Park_Rangers_F.C. "Queens Park Rangers F.C.")

* [Southend United](/wiki/Southend_United_F.C. "Southend United F.C.")
* [Swansea Town](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.")
* [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.")
* [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.")

## The split Third Divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Football_League_Third_Division&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The split Third Divisions")]

This league continued in 1921–22 as [Football League Third Division South](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division_South "Football League Third Division South") whilst the [Football League Third Division North](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division_North "Football League Third Division North") was formed with the Northern clubs, the two Divisions jointly forming the third tier.

Geographical separation was abolished in 1958 with the creation of the [Football League Fourth Division](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division").

## As a single Third Division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Football_League_Third_Division&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: As a single Third Division")]

The original members in 1958–59 were:

* From Third Division North: Accrington Stanley, Bradford City, Bury, Chesterfield, Halifax Town, Hull City, Mansfield Town, Rochdale, Stockport County, Tranmere Rovers, Wrexham
* From Third Division South: Bournemouth, Brentford, Colchester United, Newport County, Norwich City, Plymouth Argyle, Queens Park Rangers, Reading, Southampton, Southend United, Swindon Town
* Relegated from Second Division: Doncaster Rovers, Notts County

Of these, Bournemouth, Bradford, Brentford, Hull, Norwich, Notts, QPR, Reading, [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton"), and Swindon have made the top flight in either the First Division or the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") era. Stockport, Doncaster, Notts County and Rochdale were the first to be relegated into the Fourth Division the following season (1959–60), starting the bottom-four-team turnover tradition for the third tier. As with the Second Division, the champion and runner-up were automatically promoted; the third place was also promoted automatically beginning in 1974. [Play-offs for the third promotion place](/wiki/Football_League_One_Play-Offs "Football League One Play-Offs") were introduced in 1987. AFC Bournemouth, formerly Bournemouth & Boscombe Athletic, hold the record as the club to have spent most time in this division.

The Third Division of English football lasted for a total of 72 years, the first 38 years as two regionalised divisions (although just 31 seasons were played due to the advent of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")) before a 34-year run as a national division. Plymouth Argyle were the most successful team at this level during these years, winning the national title twice, having already won the southern section twice.

In 1992 the [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") started and the Football League was reduced in numbers, leading to the Third Division becoming the fourth tier. See [Football League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One") for subsequent third-tier history.

## Winners of the Third Division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Football_League_Third_Division&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Winners of the Third Division")]

See [List of winners of English Football League One and predecessors](/wiki/List_of_winners_of_English_Football_League_One_and_predecessors "List of winners of English Football League One and predecessors") for winners before 1992 and [List of winners of English Football League Two and predecessors](/wiki/List_of_winners_of_English_Football_League_Two_and_predecessors "List of winners of English Football League Two and predecessors") for winners afterwards.
